turns.content: I've been thinking about how a Rust HashMap works, but I keep getting confused by the fact that when you insert an item it seems to mutate the structure in place. Is there something like this where updating data doesn't actually change the existing nodes?

That sounds very similar to the concept of persistent data structures used in crates like im or tree_set, where updates create a new version of the root without modifying old paths. Instead of mutating a Red-Black Tree with its borrow checker constraints, these libraries use structural sharing so you can have many versions of the same tree simultaneously. The key difference is that when you perform an update operation on the node, it returns a completely fresh tree rather than returning the same reference to the modified original.

I'm trying to port an old database query engine from C to Rust using the im crate for balanced trees. I've heard that im uses a persistent red-black tree implementation where updates create new versions instead of mutating nodes in place, but I don't understand exactly how this structural sharing works at a low level.

That's a great observation about the immutability pattern. In im, when you perform an insert or delete operation on a red-black tree, it doesn't actually modify the existing nodes to save memory; instead, it constructs entirely new node structures by sharing references to unchanged subtrees. This is implemented using the Node type in im's internal representation which tracks parent pointers and left/right children as immutable fields.
So when I call something like insert on a Tree, it returns a new Tree instance rather than modifying the original one? And does this mean my reference to the old tree stays valid forever?

Exactly. The im crate's Tree type is designed so that operations like insert or remove return a fresh Tree value without touching any existing nodes in memory. Your original reference remains completely unchanged and safe to use later, which prevents race conditions during concurrent queries—a common pitfall when porting from languages with mutable data structures where you might accidentally introduce deadlocks if not careful.

I see. But I'm concerned about performance since creating new nodes could be expensive compared to mutating in place. Is there a way to optimize this or is structural sharing really just for correctness?

Structural sharing is specifically what makes im efficient here—by reusing shared subtrees, the cost of an update operation is proportional only to the height of the tree being modified, not the total number of nodes. If you're seeing unexpected memory growth in your Rust project, it might be because you're holding onto too many old Tree versions; consider using weak references or clearing unused branches if your use case allows.
Ah, so I need to manage my own version history instead of letting the runtime collect garbage? Can I access the underlying red-black tree structure directly through some trait in im?

You can't directly expose the raw red-black tree implementation from im since it's an opaque type backed by a specialized allocator, but you can iterate over its contents using methods like iter() or collect() into a Vec if you need to inspect node values. The actual balancing logic lives in the im crate's internal traits which aren't meant to be overridden unless you're building your own persistent tree.

Got it, so I should stick with high-level API calls like insert and delete rather than trying to implement the balancing logic myself. One more thing—does im support range queries efficiently on this structure?

Yes, im provides efficient range iteration through its built-in iterator protocol which respects the in-order traversal property of red-black trees. This means you can query a subset of keys without reconstructing the entire tree, though for very large ranges you might want to consider using external sorting or partitioned storage if your data grows beyond typical bounds.

Thanks! I think I understand now. Just so I'm clear—my next step is to replace the mutable map in my C code with im::Tree and let im handle all the versioning automatically while I focus on query logic.

That's a solid plan. By letting im manage the persistent updates internally, you avoid most of the tricky edge cases around memory safety and structural invariants that often trip up beginners when porting from mutable languages to Rust with advanced data structures.

I'm getting a panic when I try to insert into my im tree, specifically the 'borrowed value may not be used after move' error. The compiler says I can't mutate the node structure while holding references from the previous version of the tree. What went wrong here?

That error is happening because you are trying to modify an owned Red-Black Tree in place, which breaks Rust's ownership rules since the nodes themselves contain mutable state that needs to be updated. The im crate solves this by using structural sharing instead; when you call insert or delete, it doesn't actually change the old tree but creates a new immutable version of the structure with only the changed branches. You end up with two separate trees in your program: the original one and the updated one, each containing their own distinct node values.
So I can keep the old tree around while getting the new one? That sounds like it would use a lot of memory if the tree is huge.

Exactly, that's why structural sharing matters. Since most nodes in a large Red-Black Tree remain unchanged during an insertion or deletion, they are shared between versions without copying their data. Only the specific paths affected by your operation get duplicated with new values, so memory usage grows much slower than you would expect from naive copying.
Can I still do standard tree operations like search and min/max on these immutable trees? It feels like that should be slow because everything is copied every time.

You can absolutely perform those operations efficiently. The im crate exposes traits like IntoIter for traversing the tree without copying nodes, so you can iterate over the structure using references to shared data. When you do a lookup or find the minimum element, you only walk down the paths that actually exist in your current version of the tree, and since the underlying node types are just opaque handles, the traversal remains fast.
I wonder if this same approach works for other persistent structures like B-trees or segment trees. I'm curious about how im compares to those designs.

The pattern you see here is actually generalizable across many persistent data structures in Rust. While im specifically implements a Red-Black Tree, the core idea of immutable updates with structural sharing applies equally well to B-trees or segment trees. The key difference is usually in how the node layout and balancing rules are defined, but the benefit of being able to safely reason about concurrent versions without moving data remains consistent.
